input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to benchmark three methods (AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, UniqueWordCount) from TextAnalyzer.cs to compare .NET 9 vs .NET 10 performance. Let me start by exploring the codebase and checking what .NET versions are available.

data.content: Good — both .NET 9 and .NET 10 SDKs/runtimes are available. Let me read the source files.
data.reasoningText: Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. Let me read the source files.

Now I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that targets both net9.0 and net10.0, benchmarks the three methods, and runs. Let me create a benchmark project.

I'll create a benchmark project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0. BenchmarkDotNet supports this with `--runtimes` flag or through configuration. The approach:

1. Create a benchmark project that references the TextAnalyzer source
2. Use BenchmarkDotNet with multi-targeting
3. Run it

Actually, for BenchmarkDotNet to compare across runtimes, the benchmark project itself should multi-target, and then use `[SimpleJob]` attributes or config to run on both. Let me set this up.


I'll have the benchmark project target both runtimes and include the TextAnalyzer source directly, then use BenchmarkDotNet's `--runtimes` option to compare performance across net9.0 and net10.0.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-12c8bcd727384c0e962f64116cd58eef/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-12c8bcd727384c0e962f64116cd58eef/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-12c8bcd727384c0e962f64116cd58eef/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,13 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't support `net10.0` yet. I'll write a manual benchmark instead using `Stopwatch` that can run under both runtimes.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet doesn't recognize `net10.0` as a runtime. This version of BenchmarkDotNet (0.14.x) doesn't support .NET 10. I need a different approach. Let me use `[SimpleJob]` attributes with specific runtime monikers, or use a manual benchmarking approach instead.

Actually, let me check what version of BenchmarkDotNet was installed. Since net10.0 isn't supported, I'll take a simpler approach: write a manual benchmark that runs with both frameworks and measures performance directly using Stopwatch.

This will be more reliable given the constraints.

data.content: The high stddev suggests system load is causing outliers. Let me re-run once more with a quick sanity check using median as the stable metric:

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-12c8bcd727384c0e962f64116cd58eef/Benchmarks && dotnet run -c Release --framework net9.0 2>&1 && echo "===" && dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 2>&1

data.reasoningText:
The standard deviations are extremely high relative to the means, which indicates the machine is under heavy load and there are significant outliers. The medians are more reliable in this case. Let me re-run with more warmup and perhaps use percentile-based statistics. Actually, given the user said "this machine may be under load from other processes", the high stddev is expected. The median is the more reliable metric here.

Let me present the results using median as the primary comparison metric since it's more robust to outliers from system load.

Let me do a second run to verify consistency, or just present what we have. The user said not to spend more than 10 minutes. Let me just present the results.


I could run this again with higher warmup iterations and process priority adjustments to stabilize the numbers, but the median-based comparison already shows a clear pattern: AverageWordLength is about 4.2x faster in net10, WordLengthHistogram is roughly 3.4x faster, and UniqueWordCount shows minimal difference between the two versions.
